questions.question: The correct order of increasing thermal stability of K2CO3, MgCO3, CaCO3 and BeCO3 is:
questions.explanation:
In all cases, for a particular set of e.g. Group 1 or Group 2 compounds, the thermal stability increases down the group as the ionic radius of the cation increases, and its polarising power decreases.
Group 1 compounds tend to be more thermally stable than group 2 compounds because the cation has a smaller charge and a larger ionic radius, and so a lower polarising power, particularly when adjacent metals on the same period are compared.
Hence, the order of increasing thermal stability of K2CO3 , MgCO3, CaCO3 and BeCO3 is BeCO3 < MgCO3 < CaCO3 < K2CO3.

questions.question: Property of the alkaline earth metals that increases with their atomic number:

options.option: solubility of their sulphates in water
solubility of their hydroxides in water

questions.explanation:
As we move down the group in the alkaline earth metals:

  • Ionization energy decreases due to increasing atomic size.
  • Electronegativity decreases for the same reason.
  • Solubility of sulphates decreases (e.g., BaSO₄ is much less soluble than MgSO₄).
  • But, the solubility of hydroxides like Mg(OH)2 to Ba(OH)2 increases.

This is because the lattice energy decreases more rapidly than the hydration energy as size increases, favoring solubility.
So, solubility of their hydroxides in water increases with atomic number.

questions.question: The reducing power of a metal depends on various factors. Suggest the factor which makes Li, the strongest reducing agent in aqueous solution.
questions.explanation:
Lithium (Li) is the strongest reducing agent in aqueous solution because of its extremely high hydration enthalpy.

  • When Li loses an electron to form Li⁺, the small ion with high charge density gets very strongly hydrated in water.
  • This large negative hydration enthalpy compensates for its high ionization energy.
  • As a result, the overall reduction potential becomes highly negative, making Li a very strong reducing agent.

So, hydration enthalpy is the dominant factor here.

Since the mobility of ion is inversely proportional to the size of their hydrated ions, therefore, amongst the alkali metal ions, lithium has the lowest ionic mobility.

questions.question: The sequence of ionic mobility in aqueous solution is:

Consequently, the radii of the hydrated ion decrease from Li+ to Cs+.
Hence the ionic conductance of these hydrated ions increases from Li+ to Cs+.

questions.question:

Property Na K
(i) Ionization enthalpy (kJ mol-1) 496 419
(ii) Hydration enthalpy (kJ mol-1) -406 -330
(iii) Ionic radius (pm) 102 138

questions.question: Which of the following represents the correct order of metallic character of the given elements?

questions.question: The compound which does not exist is

The stability of the lattice of a ionic compound depends on the size difference of the cation and anion. Less the difference of the cation and anion results higher stability in the lattice of a ionic compound.

{tex}4 {Na}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {Na}_{2} {O}{/tex}
If enough excess oxygen is present, sodium oxide reacts with oxygen to produce sodium peroxide at temperatures of {tex}350-450^{\circ} {C}{/tex};
{tex}2 {Na}_{2} {O}+{O}_{2} \longleftrightarrow 2 {Na}_{2} {O}_{2}{/tex}
If sodium peroxide is reacted with an even larger excess of oxygen at high pressure, the result is sodium superoxide ({tex}{NaO}_{2}{/tex});
{tex}{Na}_{2} {O}_{2}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {NaO}_{2}{/tex}. There cannot be any why for this case.

So due to the presence of unpaired electron, it behaves as free radical. As we know that a molecule containing an unpaired electron is paramagnetic in nature. So KO2 is paramagnetic in nature because of one unpaired electron in {tex}\pi{/tex}*2p molecular orbital.

Reaction: Ca + 2H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ca(OH)2 + H2

Whereas, K gives H2 while KO2 gives O2 and H2O2.
2K + 2H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2KOH + H2

2Na + 2H2O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaOH + H2
Na2O2 + 2H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NaOH + H2O2
Likewise Ba gives H2 while BaO2 gives H2O2.

questions.question: In the preparation of sodium carbonate the by-products formed are:
questions.explanation:
Sodium carbonate is generally prepared by Solvay Process. First ammonium bicarbonate is prepared by passing CO2 to a concentrated solution of sodium chloride saturated with ammonia, where ammonium carbonate followed by ammonium hydrogen carbonate is formed. The equations for the complete process may be written as : 
{tex}2NH_3+H_2O+CO_2\to(NH_4)_2CO_3{/tex}
{tex}(NH_4)_2CO_3+H_2O+CO_2\to2NH_4HCO_3{/tex}
{tex}NH_4HCO_3+NaCl\to NH_4Cl+NaHCO_3{/tex}
Sodium hydrogen carbonate crystal separates. These are heated to give sodium carbonate. 
{tex}2NaHCO_3\to Na_2CO_3+CO_2+H_2O{/tex}
In this process, NH3 is recovered when the solution containing NH4Cl is treated with Ca(OH)2. Calcium chloride is obtained as a by-product.
{tex}2NH_4Cl+Ca(OH)_2\to 2NH_3 + CaCl_2+H_2O{/tex}

questions.question: Lime reacts exothermally with water to give '{tex}A{/tex}' which has low solubility in water. Aqueous solution of '{tex}A{/tex}' is often used for the test of {tex}C O_{2}{/tex}. A test in which insoluble {tex}B{/tex} is formed. If {tex}B{/tex} is further reacted with {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} then soluble compound is formed. '{tex}A{/tex}' is
questions.explanation:
When water is added to calcium oxide, it forms calcium hydroxide. During this process, hissing sound is observed and also a large amount of heat is evolved which converts water into steam. This process is termed as slaking of lime and fine powder obtained is called 'slaked lime'. The reaction is:
{tex}{CaO}+{H}_{2} {O} \rightarrow {Ca}({OH})_{2}{/tex}
When slaked lime is added in water it turns into a suspension which is called "milk of lime". After some time the solution becomes clear and is known as "lime water". So, on above arguments we can say that reaction of water with slaked lime is an 'exothermic' reaction. Also, from above it is cleared that calcium oxide and water react to form 'calcium hydroxide or slaked lime'. Note: It should be remembered that when carbon dioxide is passed through lime water, it turns milky due to formation of calcium carbonate. This reaction is used as a test to identify carbon dioxide gas. The reaction is:
{tex}{Ca}({OH})_{2}+{CO}_{2} \rightarrow {CaCO}_{3} \downarrow+{H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
When excess of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} is passed in above reaction, the milkiness disappears due to the formation of soluble calcium bicarbonate. The reaction is:
{tex}{\mathrm{CaCO}}_3+{\mathrm{CO}}_2+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\rightarrow\underset{(\mathrm{soluble})}{\mathrm{Ca}{\left({\mathrm{HCO}}_3\right)}_2}{/tex}

questions.question: The correct sequence of thermal stability of the following carbonates is
questions.explanation:
The correct sequence of thermal stability of carbonates is MgCO3 < CaCO3 < SrCO3 < BaCO3
On moving down the group, i.e. from Mg to Ba, atomic radius generally increases. It is due to the addition of the shell. As a result, the atomic size increases. {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} is a large anion. Hence, more stabilised by Ba2+ (large cation) and less stabilised by Mg2+. Therefore, BaCO3 has the highest thermal stability followed by SrCO3, CaCO3 and MgCO3.

questions.question: The structures of beryllium chloride in the solid state and vapour phase, respectively are
questions.explanation: The structures of beryllium chloride in the solid-state and vapour phase, respectively are dimeric and chain. In the vapour phase at above 900°C, BeCl2 is monomeric having a linear structure Cl — Be— Cl. The bonding in BeCl2 is covalent and Be atom accommodates 2 + 2 = 4 electrons in the two sp-hybrid orbitals. Below 900°C, beryllium chloride in vapour phase exists as a mixture of monomer BeCl2 and dimer Be2Cl4.

X = Be, Y = Ba

= 3.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 molecules
BaBr2 = {tex}10 \mathrm{~g} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{297 \mathrm{~g}} \times \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \text { molecules }}{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{/tex}
= 2.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 molecules
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Excess molecules = 3.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 - 2.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
= 1.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 molecules

questions.question: Which of the following has lowest density?

Density depends on mass and volume, and for gases under the same conditions (like STP), lighter molecules mean lower density.
Let's compare molar masses:

  • Hydrogen (H₂) → ~2 g/mol
  • Neon (Ne) → ~20 g/mol
  • Oxygen (O₂) → ~32 g/mol
  • Carbon (as graphite) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ~12 g/mol (but not a gas at room temperature)

Among the gases listed, hydrogen has the lowest molar mass and therefore the lowest density.
Hydrogen is the lightest and has the lowest density.

questions.question: The covalent alkaline earth metal halide (X = Cl, Br, I) is
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
Alkaline earth metals contain bipositive (H2+) ions in their compounds.
So, here

  1. Charge on cation, i.e. + 2 is constant.
  2. Halide present (X-) is also constant.

So, the covalent character depends on the size of alkaline earth metal. As we move down the group, the size of metal ion increases.
Be2+ < Mg2+ < Ca2+ < Sr2+ < Ba2+
So, Be2+ readily forms covalent compounds like BeX2, because of very high positive charge density over its small size, so that it readily polarises anionic spherical electron cloud.

questions.question: The amphoteric hydroxide is
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
For group-2 metal hydroxides, basicity increases down the group, as: Be(OH)2 < Mg(OH)2 < Ca(OH)2 < Sr(OH)2 < Ba(OH)2
This is because as the size of metal atom increases, M—OH bond length increases or M—OH bond becomes weaker thus readily breaks to release OH- ions which are responsible for the basicity of these solutions.
But Be(OH)2 shows amphoteric (basic as well as acidic) character as it reacts with acid and alkali both which is shown in the following reactions.
Be(OH)2 as a base :
Be(OH)2 + 2HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} BeCl2 + 2H2O
Be(OH)2 as an acid:
Be(OH)2 + 2NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2[Be(OH)4]

questions.question: The alkaline earth metal nitrate that does not crystallise with water molecules, is
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
A saturated aqueous solution of {tex}\stackrel{I}{M}{/tex} (NO3)2 on crystallisation will produce hydrated crystal, {tex}\stackrel{II}{M}{/tex} (NO3)2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} nH2O only when hydration enthalpy {tex}\left(\Delta H^{\circ} \mathrm{hyd}\right){/tex} of M2+ ion will be appreciably more negative.
Hydration of an ion depends on its size. Smaller the size of an ion, higher will be its charge density and as a result, it will remain more solvated (hydrated) through ion-dipole interaction.
Size of group-2 metal ions increases on going down the group. So, their ability to form hydrated crystals follows the order:
Be2+ >> Mg2+ >> Ca2+ >> Sr2+ >> Ba2+
Thus, Ba(NO3)2 is slightly or almost insoluble in water.

questions.question: Which one of the following alkaline earth metal sulphates has its hydration enthalpy greater than its lattice enthalpy?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
As we move down the group, the size of metal increases. Be has lower size while {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} has a bigger size, that’s why BeSO4 breaks easily and lattice energy becomes smaller but due to lower size of Be, water molecules are gathered around and hence hydration energy increases. On the other hand, the rest of the metals, i.e. Ca, Ba, Sr have a bigger size and that’s why lattice energy is greater than hydration energy.
The time-saving technique in the question of finding hydration energy only checks the size of the atom. Smaller sized atom has more hydration energy. Thus, in this question Be is placed uppermost in the group has a lesser size and not comparable with the size of sulphates. Hence, BeSO4 is the right response.

questions.explanation: PbO2

questions.question: The solubility in water of sulphate down the Be group is Be > Mg > Ca > Sr > Ba. This is due to:

options.option: high heat of solvation for smaller ions like Be^2+

questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: As we move down the group from BeSO4 to BaSO4 the enthalpy of hydration of the positive ion becomes smaller due to an increase in ionic size. Salts of heavier metal ions are less soluble than those of lighter ions.

questions.question: In which of the following the hydration energy is higher than the lattice energy?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
When hydration energy exceeds lattice energy, the compound becomes soluble in water. The solubility of alkaline earth metal sulphates decreases in the order
{tex}\underset{\mathrm{very}}{{\mathrm{BeSO}}_4}>\underset{\mathrm{high}}{{\mathrm{MgSO}}_4}>\underset{\mathrm{soluble}}{\underset{\mathrm{sparingly}}{{\mathrm{CaSO}}_4}}>\underbrace{{\mathrm{SrSO}}_4>{\mathrm{BaSO}}_4>{\mathrm{RaSO}}_4}_{\mathrm{almost}\;\operatorname{insoluble}}{/tex}
The solubilities of BeSO4 and MgSO4 are due to high energy of solvation of smaller Be2+ and Mg2+ ions.

questions.question: Equimolar solutions of the following were prepared in water separately. Which one of the solutions will record the highest pH?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
Equimolar solutions of the given chlorides when prepared in water forms their respective hydroxides.
Be(OH)2 is amphoteric, but the hydroxides of other alkaline earth metals are basic. The basic strength increases down the group. Hence higher the basic character higher will be the pH.

questions.question: Which of the following compounds has the lowest melting point?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: As the covalent character in the compound increases and the ionic character decreases, the melting point of the compound decreases. So, Cal2 has the highest covalent character and the lowest melting point.

questions.question: Solubility of the alkaline earth metal sulphates in water decreases in the sequence:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: Solubility of alkaline earth metal sulphates decreases down the group because hydration energy decreases.

questions.question:
20.0 g of a magnesium carbonate sample decomposes on heating to give carbon dioxide and 8.0 g magnesium oxide. What will be the percentage purity of magnesium carbonate in the sample?
(At. wt. of Mg = 24)
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
{tex}{\underset{84\;g}{{\mathrm{MgCO}}_{3(s)}}\xrightarrow\Delta}{\mathrm{MgO}}_{(s)}+\underset{40\;g}{{\mathrm{CO}}_{2(g)}}{/tex}
84 g of MgCO3 {tex}\equiv{/tex} 40 g of MgO
{tex}\therefore \ 20 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}=\frac{40}{84} \times 20{/tex}
= 9.52 g of MgO
Actual yield = 8 g og MgO
{tex}\therefore \quad \% \text { purity }=\frac{8}{9.52} \times 100{/tex} = 84%

questions.question: If A is the metallic salt, then the white ppt. of D must be of:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals

questions.question: Which of the carbonates given below is unstable in air and is kept in CO2 atmosphere to avoid decomposition.
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: BeCO3 is unstable can be kept only in the atmosphere of CO2. It is unstable due to the strong polarizing effect of the small size of Be2+ ion.

questions.question: Which of the following is an amphoteric hydroxide?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
Be(OH)2 amphoteric in nature, since it can react both with acid and base.
Be(OH)2 + 2HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BeCl2 + 2H2O
Be(OH)2 + 2NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na2[Be(OH)4]

questions.question: The ionisation energy will be maximum for the process:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
The ionization energy will be maximum for the process, Be {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Be2+. Smaller is the atom, more is the energy needed to remove an electron, i.e., ionization energy. Also, the removal of two-electron needs more energy.
The atomic number of Be is 4. To convert into Be2+, two electrons are released from 2s, which are tightly attached to the nucleus, thus, it requires a large amount of ionization energy.

questions.question: The correct order of C - O bond length is:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: CO < CO2 < {tex}CO_3^{2-}{/tex}

questions.question: The CORRECT order of thermal stability of hydroxides is:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: The amount of heating required depends on the extent of polarisation. More polarization required less energy. The smaller the positive ion is, the higher the charge density, and the greater effect it will have on the hydroxide ion. As the positive ions get larger down the group, they affect on the hydroxide ions. Therefore, the hydroxides become more thermally stable down the group.

questions.question: The CORRECT order of the solubility of alkaline-earth metal sulphates in water is
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: Solubility in water BeSO4 > MgSO4 > CaSO4 > SrSO4 > BaSO4

questions.question: Which of the alkaline earth metal halides given below is essentially covalent in nature?
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
Covalent character increases, when the cation has small size and high charge density.
Among all these, Mg2+ size is smallest, so MgCl2 tends to be more covalent in nature.

questions.question: Among the sulphates of alkaline earth metals, the solubilities of BeSO4 and MgSO4 in water, respectively, are:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation: BeSO4 and MgSO4 are readily soluble in water due to greater hydration enthalpies of Be2+ and Mg2+ ions, dominate over their lattice enthalpies and therefore their sulphates are highly soluble.

questions.question: An alkaline earth metal M readily forms water-soluble sulphate and water-insoluble hydroxide. Its oxide MO is very stable to heat and does not have rock-salt structure. M is:
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals
questions.explanation:
Solubility of BeSO4 is highest among the given metal sulphates
BeSO4 > MgSO4 > CaSO4 > SrSO4 > BaSO4
Solubility order for hydroxide is
Be(OH)2 < Mg(OH)2 < Ca(OH) < Sr(OH)2 < Ba(OH)2
Thus BeSO4 is soluble and Be(OH)2 is insoluble.
BeO does not form a rock salt like structure.
In the solid-state, it adopts the hexagonal neurtzite structure form while in the vapour phase, it is present as discrete diatomic covalent molecules.

questions.question: A substance absorbs CO2 and violently reacts with water. The substance is
questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals

CaO + H2O {tex}\xrightarrow{{Hissing sound}} {/tex} Ca(OH)2 + Heat
CaO + CO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCO3

questions.topicName: General Characteristics of Compounds of The Alkaline Earth Metals

questions.question: In comparison to boron, beryllium has

options.option: lesser nuclear charge and greater first ionisation enthalpy
greater nuclear charge and greater first ionisation enthalpy
greater nuclear charge and lesser first ionisation enthalpy
lesser nuclear charge and lesser first ionisation enthalpy

questions.explanation:
In comparison to boron, beryllium has lesser nuclear charge and greater first ionisation enthalpy.
Electronic configuration of Be(4) = 1s2, 2s2
It possess completely filled s-orbitals. Hence, high amount of energy is required to pull the electron from the gaseous atom. Beryllium (4) lies left to the boron (5) and on moving from left to right an electron is added due to which nuclear charge increases from Be to B.

questions.question: The metal used for making X-ray tube window is
questions.explanation:
Among the four elements given, Na, Be, Mg and Ca, Be has highest IE value because of its smallest size and 2s2 valence shell configuration.
So, X-ray cannot cause ionisation from the material used, i.e. Be in the tube window, which may cause interference in the study.

questions.question: Which of the following has a tendency for covalent compound formation?
questions.explanation:
Mg has a greater covalent compound forming tendency than Na, Ca, and Ba.
This is due to the small size and strong positive nuclear charge attraction due to which ion formation involves higher energies.

questions.question:
{tex}{Be}({OH})_{2}{/tex} reacts with {tex}{Sr}({OH})_{2}{/tex} to yield an ionic salt.
Choose the incorrect option related to this reaction from the following:

options.option: Be is tetrahedrally coordinated in the ionic salt.
Both Sr and Be elements are present in the ionic salt.
The reaction is an example of acid - base neutralization reaction.
The element Be is present in the cationic part of the ionic salt.

questions.explanation:
{tex}{Be}({OH})_{2}{/tex} is amphoteric in nature which can react with both acidic as well as basic substance.
{tex}\underset{\text { acid }}{{Be}({OH})_{2}}+\underset{\text { base }}{{Sr}({OH})_{2}} \rightarrow \underset{\text { salt }}{{Sr}\left[{Br}({OH})_{4}\right]}{/tex}
Be contains vacant orbital in {tex}{Be}({OH})_{2}{/tex} and act as an {tex}{e}^{-}{/tex}deficient compound as well as lewis acid 08 it accepts {tex}{e}^{-}{/tex}pair in the form of {tex}{OH}^{-}{/tex}from {tex}{Sr}({OH})_{2}{/tex} to form salt.
Here in the {tex}{Sr}\left[{Be}({OH})_{4}\right]{/tex} salt, element Be is present in the anionic part of the salt not in the cationic part.
{tex}{Sr}\left[{Be}({OH})_{4}\right] \rightarrow {Sr}^{2+}+\left[{Be}({OH})_{4}\right]^{2-}{/tex}

questions.explanation: {tex}{A}_{0.95} {O}{/tex} has a metal deficiency defect due to the absence of metal ions where the metal {tex}\operatorname{ion}\left({A}^{2+}\right){/tex} is absent from its lattice site. The electrical neutrality is maintained by the presence of metal ions in a higher oxidation state {tex}\left({A}^{3+}\right){/tex}. Where {tex}3 {~A}^{2+}{/tex} are seplaud by {tex}{A}^{3+}{/tex} and these one vacaul site per pair of {tex}{A}^{3+}{/tex} is created.

questions.question: The temporary hardness of a water sample is due to compound X . Boiling this sample converts X to compound Y.X and Y, respectively, are
questions.explanation:
The temporary hardness of a water sample is due to compound X [i.e. Mg(HCO3)2]. Boiling of this sample converts X[i.e. Mg(HCO3)2] to compound y[i.e. Mg(OH)2]. Generally, temporary hardness is due to presence of magnesium and calcium hydrogen carbonates. It can be removed by boiling. During boiling, the soluble Mg(HCO3)2 is converted into insoluble Mg(OH)2 and Ca(HCO3)2 changed to insoluble CaCO3 . These precipitates can be removed by Alteration.
{tex}\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{\text { Heating }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \downarrow+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{\text { Heating }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \downarrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}

questions.question: The hardness of a water sample (in terms of equivalents of CaCO3) containing is (Molar mass of CaSO4 = 136 g mol-1)

Given, molarity = 10-3 M
i.e. 1000 mL of solution contains 10-3 mole of CaCo3.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Hardness of water = ppm of CaCO3
{tex}=\frac{10^{-3} \times 1000}{1000} \times 10^{6}{/tex}
= 100 ppm

questions.explanation:

questions.question: A solid compound X on heating gives CO2 gas and a residue. The residue mixed with water forms Y. On passing an excess of CO2 through Y in water, a clear solution Z is obtained. On boiling Z, compound X is reformed. The compound X is:

questions.question: For a good quality cement, the ratio of lime to the total of the oxides of {tex}{Si}, {Al}{/tex} and Fe should be as close as to
questions.explanation:
The raw materials required for the manufacture of cement are lime stone, stone and clay. Lime stone or calcium carbonate {tex}\left({CaCO}_{3}\right){/tex} provides calcium oxide. (CaO) Clay is hydrated aluminium silicate, {tex}\left({Al}_{2} {O}_{3} \cdot 2 {SiO}_{2} \cdot 2 {H}_{2} {O}\right){/tex} and it provides alumina as well as silica. A small amount of gypsum, {tex}{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} is also required. It is added in calculated quantity in order to adjust the rate of setting of cement.
Manufacture: Cement is made by strongly heating a mixture of lime stone and clay in a rotatory kiln. Lime stone and clay are finely powdered and a little water is added to get a thick paste called slurry. The slurry is fed into a rotatory kiln from the top through the hopper. The hot gases produce a temperature of about {tex}1770-1870 {~K}{/tex} in the kiln. At this high temperature the lime stone and clay present in slimy combine to form cement in the form of small pieces called clinker. This clinker is mixed with {tex}2-3 \%{/tex} by weight of gypsum ({tex}{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}) to regulate the setting time and is then ground to an exceedingly fine powder.
Limestone + Clay {tex}\xrightarrow[\text { Clinker }]{1770-1870 {~K}}{/tex} Cement {tex}+{CO}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}
When mixed with water the cement reacts to form gelatinous mass which sets to a hard mass when three dimensional cross lines are formed between silica oxygen silica and silica oxygen aluminium as ...Si-O-Si... and Si-O-Al...
Composition of cement:
{tex}{CaO}=50-60 \%{/tex}
{tex}{SiO}_{2}=20-25 \%{/tex}
{tex}{Al}_{2} {O}_{3}=5-10 \%{/tex}
{tex}{MgO}=2-3 \%{/tex}
{tex}{Fe}_{2} {O}_{3}=1-2 \%{/tex}
{tex}{SO}_{3}=1-2 \%{/tex}
For a good quality cement the ratio of silica ({tex}{SiO}_{2}{/tex}) and alumina {tex}\left({Al}_{2} {O}_{3}\right){/tex} should be between 2.5 to 4.0. Similarly the ratio of lime (CaO) to the total oxide mixtures consisting of {tex}{SiO}_{2}, {Al}_{2} {O}_{3}{/tex} and {tex}{Fe}_{2} {O}_{3}{/tex} should be roughly {tex}2: 1: 1{/tex}, If lime is in excess, the cement cracks during setting. On the other hand, if lime is less than the required, the cement is weak in strength. Therefore, a proper composition of cement must be maintained to get cement of good quality.

questions.question: What is the purpose of adding gypsum to cement?

questions.explanation: Gypsum ({tex}{CaSO}_{4} \cdot 2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}) plays an important role in controlling the rate of hardening of the cement. During cement manufacturing process upon cooling of clinker a small amount of gypsum is added during the final grinding process. Gypsum controls the setting of the cement and if not added the cement will set immediately leaving no time for concrete placing.
